critique.summary: The article introduces Sonar, a command-line interface (CLI) tool designed to identify and manage processes listening on localhost ports. It provides features such as displaying Docker container details, resource usage, killing processes by port, viewing logs, and attaching to services, aiming to simplify port management for developers.
mainPoints: Sonar lists all processes listening on localhost ports, including Docker container names and Compose projects.
It allows users to kill processes, tail logs, shell into containers, and perform other management tasks by specifying port numbers.
The tool supports features like resource usage statistics, health checks, remote scanning via SSH, and port mapping.
Sonar is installable via a shell script and supports shell completions for easier use.
It is compatible with macOS and Linux platforms, using system utilities like lsof and ss.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Sonar's functionality are consistent with the information available on its official GitHub repository. The described features such as listing ports, showing Docker container details, killing processes by port, and viewing logs align with the documented capabilities of the tool. The installation instructions and supported platforms also match the official documentation. There is no indication of exaggerated or false claims.
considerations: The article does not mention potential security implications of using a tool that can kill processes and attach shells remotely, which could be significant in multi-user or production environments.
It does not discuss performance impact or limitations when running Sonar on systems with a large number of ports or containers.
The article lacks information about the tool's maintenance status or community support beyond the GitHub stars and forks.
No comparison with similar existing tools is provided to contextualize Sonar's unique advantages or disadvantages.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around a new CLI tool called Sonar designed to identify and kill processes running on localhost, addressing common developer frustrations with managing ports and orphaned processes. Users appreciate its simplicity and express interest in additional features like customizable URLs for Docker containers.

comments.author: raskrebs
comments.text:
I always have a bunch of local projects running, particularly during the weekend where I'm rarely working on one thing at a time. A big pain of mine was constantly running into port: Redis from one project blocking another, orphaned dev servers from old worktrees, Docker containers I forgot about. The usual fix is lsof -iTCP | grep ..., then figuring out what the PID actually is, then killing it. But I always forget the command, and it doesn’t really include all the information that I like.

So I built this lightweight CLI. Single binary, no dependencies. It shows everything listening on localhost with process names, Docker container info, clickable URLs etc.

Sure there are workarounds, but none that satisfied my need for a short, easily rememberable command. Also nothing really has the same satisfaction as running sonar kill 3000 — it just feels nice. I’ve already been approached by a few agent orchestration tools that have been struggling with the same thing. It's really useful when you have multiple agents running, but it's not built for just that use case, I have also find it handy when killing off all containers after a failed cleanup and so on. Also know that MCPs are dead and CLIs are the new thing in agentic coding, this might be a useful tool for Claude, particularly when a compose process exits before all containers are stopped.

Open for contributions, ideas and feedback.
comments.analysis: The author provides a detailed explanation of the problem and how the tool addresses it, including practical use cases and motivation, making this a reliable and informative comment.

comments.author: klaushardt
comments.text:
Would be nice to have a flag to customize the URL displayed for Docker containers. I connect to my host via Tailscale, but I can’t open links with localhost. It would be helpful to have a parameter that allows us to choose a network device or specify an IP address to display.

    3000    wud (getwud/wud:latest)                            wud          getwud/wud:latest                       3000    http://localhost:3000
    3001    dockhand (fnsys/dockhand:latest)                   dockhand     fnsys/dockhand:latest                   3000    http://localhost:3001
comments.analysis: This comment offers a constructive feature request based on a real-world use case, enhancing the tool's usability for users accessing hosts via networks other than localhost.

comments.author: Bradd3rs
comments.text: love this, i get tired of spamming lsof -i tcp:xxxx
comments.analysis: A brief positive endorsement expressing relief at having a simpler alternative to a common but tedious command, though it lacks detailed analysis.
